COURRENT AFFAIRS,

Sir William Goulding’s little game of foisting a fel-
- ! low-director in the Goulding Manure Co. as a Catholic
candidate for auditorship of the Great S. and W. Rail-

way was very slim. It was worthy of the Inner Guard
Sword-Bearer of the Secret Society by the name of the
Freo and Accepted Masons of Ireland.  This eminent
Freemason, it will be remembered, touted for pl:oxies
long ‘before the Report of the G. S. and W. Railway

~ was sent to the shareholders of the company of which
Mr. Pilkington, of the National Assurance Co., is a
financial ~ giant. The man who was to
been put up for—and who, it s

ow rumoured, has -left the field—the post

f auditor, besides being a Papist, a “ National ”’ Edu-
ation Commissioner, and a director of Brobhe.r ‘Gould-
xug'é» Manure Co., occupies the exalted position of
‘hairman of the Cork, Blackrock, and Passage Railway,
hich, we learn, is mot paying interest on its entire
enture Stock ; he is an ex-director of the famous Na-
ssurance Co., of which Mr. Pilkington, of the
nd W. Railway, is a financial giant ; and we hear
he went to America with the secretary of that un-

al

happy and ill-fated Corporation in order to advise on
the taking over of American risks; he was educated in
England, and so, though not desirable as an auditor of
the G 8. and W. Railway, is fully eligible to join the
Freebooter’s Football Club. We hear now from a good
source that this director in Brother Goulding’s Manure
Co. has withdrawn his candidature for auditorship on
the G. 8. and W. Railway. Probably he did not like
to face the music from Mr. Sullivan, Mr. Sexton, and
others of the Shareholders’ Committee. We now under-
stand that the auditor who was to 'create the vacancy for
Brother Goulding’s co-director in the Manure business,
is to hold on to office. We feel sure that the apprecia-
tion of Sir Willian Goulding, Inner Guard Sword-
Bearer of the Free and Accepted Masons of Treland—
that Secret Society that is such a pest in the Irish com-
mercial world—we feel sure that his appreciation of the
enlightened criticism that has been directed on the G. S.
and W. Railway and to himself, is far too strong for
publication.

Our sympathies naturally go out to anyone who makes
little of Local Government Bo auditors in this coun-
try. Mr. John Cummins, Chairman of the New Ross
Guardians, has his own views on these highly-paid gen-
tlemen ; apparently he stands in no dread of them. At
a recent meeting of the New Ross Board Mr. Cummins
proposed that the Guardians be supplied with luncheon
at future meetings. In this particular case luncheon
or no luncheon is not a question that wildly interests
us ; our interest is chiefly in the independent and sturdy
attitude of Mr. Cummins towards the auditors of the
unrepresentative and justly unpopular Local Govern-
ment Board. By way of reply to a Guardian who
raised the question of the bogey man, the auditor, Mr.
Cummins replied that if he surcharged the members of
the Board the members could pay for what they ate, and
“no thanks to the auditor, or any twopence-halfpenny
man from the Local Government Board.””  Later on
the bogey men of the L.G.B. were referred to again.
“Don’t mind those buckoes,”” said Mr. Cummins;
“if you knew all the hobbles I had with the Local
Government Board, you would not blame me for being
antagonist to them. They’d look small in your eyes as
well as mine. T’ll allow no £800 or £900 a year man
to come down from Dublin and dictate to me.””

Apart altogether from the merits of the case, we are
glad to note and applaud this sturdy, independent
spirit in relation to the officials of unrepresentative
Castle Boards on the part of a Chairman of a Board of
Guardians. This spirit ought to spread, and the more

* the popular representatives, men of local leading like

Mr. Cummins, stand up boldly to people of the Local
Government Board the bettor. Castle Government
would get a serious set back if every local governing
body had a few men of the temper of Mr. Cummins.
Unfortunately the very fact that the auditors of this
Castle Board draw some £800 a year is enough of itself
to cause many of our half-slaves to bow down to them.
‘We look forward with interest to see if any of “those
buckoes ”’ will try a fall with Mr. Cummins and his
fellow-Guardians of New Ross.

The Queen’s Collegos ought to feel pretty small in
view of the honours lists of the recent Arts Examina-
tion of the R.U.I.- Each of these Colleges draws from
the public purse a sum moderately estimated at £10,000
a year, whilst University College, Dublin, gets nothing
whatever beyond the services of fifteen Fellows of the
R.U.I. which may be estimated in terms of money as
cqual to an endowment of £4,500. The Queen’s Col-
loges have splendid buildings; the local habitation of

398

University College might not unreasonably be called a
dilapidated old building, and is quite inadequate in
floor space for the demands made upon it. For its
- £10,000, Queen’s College, Cork, turned out a total of 9

Honours at the half-yearly test, or at the rate of one

for every £556 annual endowment. Queen’s College,

Galway, produced 21, or one for about every £238 an-

nual endowment. Queen’s College, Belfast—and Bel-

fast is where all the world knows the “ wealth and intel-

ligence *’ of Ireland comes from—produced a total of 22,

or one for every £227 b5s. All we can say is that

the whole three of them ought to be ashamed of them-
selves; and it is a scandalous misuse of public money
that it should be squandered in this wasteful and un-
fruitful manner. Let us turn now to the poorly-housed

University College of Dublin that receives about

£4,600, through the Government, of public money.

That “obscurant’’ institution where Jesuits, without

any disguise whatever, may be seen prowling round any

day, that “obscurant’’ institution produced a total of

45 Honours, or at the rate of exactly one Honour for

every £50 annual endowment. If there were three

prizes awarded to the institutions that spent most on the
- average for, if we may say so, fattening an “ Honour ’’
for the R.U.I. market, the results would work out on
the last half-year’s results in this way :—

Cork Queen’s College 555
Galway Queen’s College 238
Belfast Queen’s College 227

whilst University College, Dublin, would be out of the
race altogether, for it is so economical and successful in
its methods that it produces one “ Honour ’’ for every
£50 annual grant. If, on the other hand, prizes were
offered regardless of money squandered or well spent to
the institution that produced the largest “ Honours,”
then the list would run this way : —

University College 45
Belfast Queen’s College 22
Galway Queen’s College . R ol
Cork Queen’s College 9

The Queen’s Colleges ought to bow their heads, and
Belfast Queen’s College particularly ought to feel humi-
liated that it was beaten—and badly beaten—by, of all
places in the world, a nunnery! Loreto College, Dub-
lin, produced 31 “Honours’’ against the Queen’s Col-
lege, Belfast total of 22—majority for a nunnery over
the highly-endowed College in Belfast, whera so much
of the “wealth and intelligence ”’ come from—9. If
one nunnery beat Belfast by 31 to 22 in the free and
open test in the Examination Halls of the Royal Uni-
versity, another nunnery finished level with it, for St.
Mary’s College scored also 22 “ Honours.”” These two
nunneries together totalled 53 “ Honours,”” and we find
that that much-advertised institution that is free from
Popery, Alexandra College, only produced eight
“Honours,”” and Victoria College produced a like num-
ber. Loreto and St. Mary’s, Dublin, produced 53,
whilst Alexandra and Victoria, notwithstanding their
high-sounding, royal titles, and their freedom from
““Idolatry,” only produced hetween them 16—majority

for “Idolatry,”” “superstition,”” *obscurantism,”’ only
371

“The cricket club continues to be a source of much
enjoyment to the members.”” This sentence is not, taken
from a report of the Castleknock College Union; it is
taken from the Report read at the fifty-first half-yearly
general meoting of the City of Dublin Workingmen’s
Club, 10 Wellington Quay. The subscriptions.during
the half-year ending June 30th, 1905. amounted to
£80 168, 6d., and the Bar Receipts to.£671 6s. A Bar
bill of that dimensions for a half-year is equivalent to
one of £1,342 12s. on a full year’s working. From one

’ poipt of view, at least, the club is a live, and probably
- a lively concern.

knocks last week." Here is portion of an answer which

attack was made on Mr. Magenis’s house. That gentle-
man asserts that no one has asked him to give up his
farm.”” 1t is only fair to state that Mr. Magennis says
that the police report is untrue.

Father Coleman, O.P., continues in this month’s
Irish Rosary his very interesting series of articles on
“Japan To-day.” The instalment in the current issue
deals with the religion of Japan, and with the Catholic
missionary efforts and persecutions that Japan was the
scene of.

Mr. E. J. Riordan, the Secretary of the Cork Indus-
trial Development Association, asks us to draw attention

to the fact that his Association purposes publishing a

second edition of their Irish Manufacturers’ Directory.
The first edition did not, as was stated in the preface,
contain an exhaustive list of Irish manufactures, as
many firms failed to send details of the goods they
manufactured. It is the aim of the Association that the
second edition should contain as nearly as possible an ex-
haustive list, and so they invite all manufacturers to
send particulars without delay.  Letters may be ad-
dressed to the Secretary, Mr. E. J. Riordan, at 13
Marlboro’ Street, Cork.

Very many of our readers who personally knew the
late Father Moloney will be particularly glad to hear
that the Gaelic League of London has decided to take
steps with a view, amongst other matters, of transfer-
ring the remains of the late patriot priest from the bleak
North of England to a last resting-place in the land he
loved so well, and for whose higher national progress he
worked so hard. In addition to this, it is proposed to
honour his memory by establishing annual studentships
at the Munster and Connacht Training Colleges to be
known as the Father Moloney Memorial Studentships,
and also to offer for annual competition at the Owreach-
tas a Cup to be known as the Father Moloney Memorial
Cup, and to be held for one year by the writer of the best
essay on some subject dealing with the ecclesiastical his-
tory of Treland. A special Memorial Committee is being
formed.

There has been some more Parliamentary questioning
concerning the threatened withdrawal of the grant for
Trish as an extra subject. Mr. Long, the Orangemen’s
Chief Secretary, repeated what he had said on the pre-
vious occasion, that it was decided to withdraw the pay-
ments for the teaching of Irish as an extra subject. The
National Board, he said, had arrived at the unanimous
decision that, provided the money was available for
other Irish educational purposes, the grants available
for the oxtra subjects could not be continued. M

The John Street National Schools are paying tribute
to the memory of the late Father Andorson in a very
practical way. In the course of a report, the result of
& visit to the schools by Father M‘Incrney and Mr.
O'Daly, we read:—The number of teachers in the
schools is nine, all of whom know Irish, and four are
fluent native speakers. The average attendance is 330,
and Irish is taught to every class from infants upwards,
inclusive. It is a noteworthy feature that the teaching
is practically entirely oral—as a living spoken language
—books being, of course, used as required by pro-
gramme.  Special attention is also given to original com-
position in Irish as home exercise. Asan ‘extra’ the
language is taught for half an hour each day. The
study of the national language receives special and prac-
tical encouragement in the shape of weekly money
prizes, etc., which are kindly supplied by the manager.
More substantial prizes such as watches, clocks, foot-
balls, etc., are distributed annually, being also supplied
by the personal generosity of the manager.”

If we have not a lively interest in the popular side
of sport, we do not overlook the importance of its eco-
nomic side. There are many things in the sporting
line which Ireland now largely imports that ought, with
advantage, be made at home.  Fishing rods were, we
learn, at one time. extensively made in Dublin, but the

¥ industry of-past years has been practically wiped out by
importations.  We understand that Mr. L. Keegan,
Upper Ormond Quay, is the only maker of these
articles now in Dublin. Mr. Keegan, who does a large
business in various sporfing requisites, recently took up

the rod-making in order to save it from extinction in

- Dublin. It is to be hoped that Irish purchasers in this

~ line will back up Mr. Keegan’s effort.

A correspondent tells us that some people are under

the impression that “ The Philosophy of Irish Ireland

- comprises articles that have already appeared in the
Leaper. This is not so.  The first of the articles ori-
ginally appeared in the New [reland Review of Decem-
ber, 1898, and the last of the series in August, 1900.
The author says in the preface to them :—* The articles

~  contain the reflections, the arguments, and register the
convictions that led up to the starting of the LEADER.”

.= The August number of Z'he Drapers’ Assistant not un-
naturally pays considerable attention to the question of
what is called the Living-in System. The Camden
Strest fire has focussed public attention on this matter.
Judging by an editorial article in our contemporary the
.drapers’ assistants feel very strongly on this system,
“that accursed system known as ‘living-in,” "’ as 7he
Drapers’ Assistant styles it.  Our contemporary says
that “nothing short of the complete abolition of the
system will satisfy those whose claims we advocate.”
The fire danger is only one of the evils complained of ;
there is the effect on health of those who “have to
~ sleep in insanitary surroundings, in overcrowded apart-
ments, in company with consumptives and, in some in-
ces, have to share the same bed. The result is in-
evitable, and is fully exemplified in what we must
escribe as a sad procession of young men and young
men who are ordered home almost daily to the coun-
1ll health, in a great many cases never to return.
ir places are filled up by other young aspirants, with
‘who, in turn, will, in a short time, be superseded
nd so the clever city trader supplies the ter-
kage which annually takes place in con-

nection with shop life.”” That is a terrible indictment.
The curtailment of social and citizen rights are also
strongly complained of.

In connection with this question we notice that in
Monday’s Independent it is stated that ¢ Messrs Gore-
van, Bros., Camden Street, have given their employees
the option of sleeping out, and.in order to meet the
extra expense entailed thereby have promised to increase
their salaries by £10 each.”

The arrangements for the Re-union during the
Oireachtas week of the past and present members of
the Gaelic League of London are now complete.  The
Re-union will take the form of a light sit-down supper
and a Ceilidh, and will be held in the D.B.C. Restau-
rant, O’Connell St., on Tuesday, 15th August. Supper
will start at 10 p.m., and the Ceilidh will continue
until 3 a.m. The Re-union, of course, will be confined
to past and present members of the London Branch, but
gentlemen may invite a lady friend and wvice versa. The
function should be a very interesting one, for a very
large number of the most prominent workers in the Gae-
lic League are, or have been, members of the League in
London.  Tickets, 2s. 6d. single, 4s. 6d. double, are
now ready, and all who intend to be present are asked
to purchase them early. They may be had on applica-
tion to An Runaire, Comh-Chruinniughadh Connartha
na Gaedhilge, Lunduin, 24 Sraid Ui Chonaill, or any
members of the Committee.

The dnglo-Celt of August 5th contains the follow-
ing:—“Mr. D. P. Moran, editor of the Dublin
LEADER, issues in book form (price 1s) six articles which
he has written since 1898 on the ‘reflections and the
arguments ’ which convinced him that a paper such as
that with which he has since been associated was neces-
sary in Ireland. Since 1900 he has made history.
Many of the metropolitan papers will deny this, but a
denial cannot get away from the fact and the policy pur-
sued by our dailies in ignoring the existence of the
little LEADER is simply childish. The journal has done
a great deal of good to the trade of Ireland, and an in-
calculable amount of harm to the dignity of that
clectro-plated West Britonism of Dublin which spends
every copper it possesses in England. In the course of
articles in the book under notice, Mr Moran dealt in
plain language with what he considered to be incon-
gruous in Irish life, and if, at times, he hit rather
severely it was no doubt because he considered a light
pat would not effect the purpose. He has made count-
less enemies in this country. We might safely say that
no man in Ireland has within five years ever reached
such a total, but then the man who does not make
encmies is akin to the man who never makes mistakes
and the man who never makes mistakes never makes
anything. Taking this as a basis, bigger things may
yel be expected from the LEADER man.

The Cookstown (Co. Tyrone) General Credit and Dis-
count Company, Ltd., is winding up. At an extra-
ordinary general meeting of the shareholders held in
July 22nd it was resolved to wind up voluntarily. The
downfall of this company is a matter principally of
local concern; but the fact that Rev. Dr. Wilson, one

_of the Commissioners of “National’’ Education, is a

director is of some general interest. This was the man
who was so eager to announce that the Commissioners
had decided to stop the fees for teaching Irish as an
extra subject.

One of the best ways to counteract what we hear
described as  the stream of immoral literature flooding
the country’’ is to set up opposing forces in the shape
of interesting wholesome publications issued at a popu-
lax price. « We are, therefore, glad to notice that Messrs.
James Duffy and Co. have begun the publication of a
sories of Catholic stories which they are issuing at the
price of 1d. each. One of the series will be published
every month in neat, handy form, suitable for Catholic

Truth boxes.

THE G. 8. & W. RAILWAY CO.

T was not without reason, though totally without jus--
tice, that Sir William Goulding, Inner Guard
Sword-Bearer of the Free and Accepted Masons of Ire-
land, and his co-directors of the Great S. and W. Rail-
way, touted for the sharcholders’ proxies long before -
they allowed the shareholders to see the report. As
carly as July 21st the directors of this company, whose
position so urgently calls for exhaustive and impartial
inquiry sent out a request for proxies by return of
post.’” It is very significant that they should have re-
quested these proxies by “return of post’’ for the
damaging Report—a Report calculated to increase the
already keen anxiety of the sharcholders—was not
placed in the hands of the shareholders until consider-
ably more than a week afterwards. Sir William Gould-
ing wanted, not without reason on his side, to make a
sort of what, in our school days, we used to call a
“black swop.”” But it was a one-sided “ black swop ’’
at that; for Sir William knew the value of the proxies
that he was asking for, whilst the shareholders were
ignorant of the Report that they were to receive in ex-
change after they had delivered up their proxies. The
whole business looks decidedly tricky. Sir William has
been trying, to use one of his own elegant phrases, to
put his finger in the eyes of the shareholders.

The shareholders of this depreciated railway com-
pany are naturally suffering from keen anxiety, an
anxiely that has been aggravated by the disastrous col-
lapse of the ill-fated National Assurance Co., of which
Frederick C. Pilkington, of the G. S. and W. Railway,
is a director; the anxiety is further aggravated by the
Report, and the unworthy and tricky attempt to secure
shareholders’ proxies before it was published.

‘We would not devote such attention to the condition
of this company if it were merely a matter concerning
the property of the individual sharcholders. The busi-
ness of shareholders and all such others is to look after
their own commercial affairs, and if they don’t do it,
they, like the unfortunate shareholders of the National
Assurance Co., have largely themselves to blame. But
this question, for many reasons, is a matter of great
national importance. Whilst we want as much Home
Rule as we can get, we are particularly impressed by the
necessity of using all the Home Rule that we possess,
and it is of great importance, with a view to developing
the latter habit, to induce the Irish public to take a
keen interest in all such institutions as railways, banks,
and so forth, that have such an effect for good or ill on
the .forbunes of the country. We are sure that the
habit of using the Home Rule we have has been con-
siderably cultivated as a result of the public attention
which, for nearly five years, we have been endeavouring
to fix on various phases of the railway questions of Ire-
land. The people in this country are sadly lacking in
that fine working capacity expressed by the phrase
“knowing their way about.”’ Irish shareholders are
too much like dumb animals wagging their tails wh
they are thrown dividends and impotently how!

THE CATHOLIC UNIVERSITY OF LOUYAIN.

Since the Catholic University was constituted on the
model of the Catholic University of Louvain, it may be
well to illustrate the aim of its founders by showing
briefly what Louvain has achieved for Belgium. The
old University of Louvain, which had been founded in
1425 by Pope Martin V., and which in the course of its
glorious career had produced some of the most famous
men of the modern world—amongst them, Iirasmus,
the humanist; Justus Lipsius, the polygraphist; Vesa-
lius, the creator of the science of anatomy ; Dodoens, the
_botanist; Riga, the physician; Mercator, the geo-
grapher; Jansenius, and many other theologians of
great name, amongst them not a few Irishmen—was
destroyed at the French Revolution. When the Revo-
lution became incarnate in the person and despotism of
Napoleon, and he in his turn was extinguished, Catho-
lic Belgium demanded back its ancient University. It
was partially restored under the Dutch Government,
which succeeded the Revolution era in Belgium. But
troubles arose from the Chair of Philosophy. Principles
were propounded which the conscience of Catholic Bel-
gium could not accept, and the storm came which had
much to do with the uprising that set Belgium freec.
In 1834 the history of the new University begins,
restored by a Pontifical Decree—Catholic in its constitu-
tion, and in all its parts. Its Rector is appointed by
the Bishops.” It has about a hundred Professors, about
one-fourth of whom.are ecclesiastics. It has no State
endowment ; it is supported by an annual contribution
of the people, as was the Catholic University of Ireland,
and by the occasional munificence of individuals. About
£20,000 a yeay is said to be its revenue. But, unlike
our Catholic University, its Degrees are recognised by
the State. The French Revolution and the Dutch
domination left a legacy after them in the form of Bel-
gian liberalism which had its own way till 1884. It
chartered three Godless Universities, at Ghent, Liege,
and Brussels. Against those, Louvain in its compara-
tive poverty had to compete; and this is the testimony
of the Belgian people to its efficiency. It began in
1834 with 86 students; the next year it had 261; in
1845 it had 809; in 1871 it had 1,045; in 1885, it had
1,700 ; in 1889, it had 1,891 ; it has now over 2,000. It
has five Faculties, namely, Theology, Law, Medicine,
Philosophy and Letters, and the Sciences. From the

. Annuaire of 1899, I find that 54 students entered for

Theology the year before, 363 for Law, 403

. for Medicine, 247 for Philosophy and Letters, and

956 for the Sciences. It had not complete autonomy
till 1876, when the mixed Commission before which the
students had to be examined for Degrees was abolished.
Since then it confers its own Degrees, which are recog-
nised by the State; and it is worthy of remal:k that its
greatest progress dates from that time when it was un-
yoked from the State and left to expand freely as a
Catholic University unfettered by outside officialdom.
The other three Universities had a free course also, un-
tramelled by catechisms, dogmas, or ecclesiastical super-
stitions of any kind; yet Louvain had been attracsiug
so many students that the others have been modifying
their secularism in recent years. The consciences of tiie
ultra-liberals were so troubled thereby that about a
dozen years ago they had a sccond University estab-
lished in Brussels, the Université Nowvelle. It did not
succeed in establishing the four faculties necessary for

obtaining the legal recognition which would give it -

power to grant its own Degrees. For 139 professors it
had 154 students. To leave the liberals no ground of
complaint, M. Schollaert, the Minister of Public In-
struction, appointed Boards for four consecutive years to
examine students presented by it for Degrees. The
candidates were found to be so deficient that M. Schol-
laert refused to have any more to do with it, and to
justify his action against the protests and charges of
clericalism made by certain liberals, he made a public
exposure of the imposture in the Senate. It died about
six years ago—from natural causes, a Coroner’s jury
would say. Not to speak of those whom it has sent to
other countries, the graduates of Louvain are to be
found in Belgium amongst the clergy, in parliament, in
the press, amongst country gentlemen, lawyers, physi-
cians, engineers, in the military and civil service, in
diplomatic and commercial life. It has sent out amongst
the people men of highest culture and truest Catholic
thought, who have leavened public sentiment with the
principles of action which should govern a Catholic
nation. And have these been leaders of obscurantism?
have they damped the aspirations of the people, or
paralysed their energy? The singular position of Bel-
gium in the world of industry contains the answer. In
1884 the Catholics drove the liberals from power. Since
then, if they have not held undisputed sway, they have
for the most part guided the destiny of the kingdom,
and its prosperity has advanced. Technical schools for
boys and girls, which were very few in 1884, have
greatly increased. Workingmen’s Aid Societies have
sprung up with remarkable rapidity. The clergy take
a leading part in this work, and have formed an Eccle-
siastical Committee with representatives in different
provinces for the promotion of the movement. There
is also the Boerenbond, or Peasant’s League, something
like our Agricultural Societies here, founded by the
Abbé Mellaerts some years ago. Its object is to pro-
mote the moral and material interests of agriculturists,
to improve land legislation, to promote agricultural co-
operation. There is the Aumoniers du Travail, a
society of priests instituted by the bishop of Liege, who
make a special study of social questions, and promote in
their parishes, as circumstances demand or opportunity
offers, economic and social institutions for the better-
ment of the people. They have established working-
men’s hostels in the great industrial centres. Then
there are two great social organisations, both with a
common object, but seeking to reach it by different
ways. One is the Federation des Cercles Catholigues,
under the leadership of M. Woeste; the other is the
Ligue Democratique Belge, under the leadership of M.
Arthur Verhaegen. The former are Conservatives ; the
latter call themselves Catholic democrats. They were
founded by the Abbé Pottier about fifteen years ago,
and have already several hundred societies and several
hundred thousand members. They profess to follow
the lines traced out by Leo XIII. in his famous En-
cyclical De Conditione Opificum. The light which has
led to those associations came from Louvain, and I

Besides the five ordinary University Faculties, Schools
of Special Studies have been established in Louvain in
recent years; and to those Ecoles Spéciales I will limit
'my observations. From 1865 to 1872, the Schools of
Arts and Manufactures, of Civil Engineering, Mining,
and Architecture, were established. The authorities
did not think a University to be a literary and scien-
tific museum meant only for show, or a mere factory
for turning out professional men ; it should be a centre
whence light, inspiration, and influence should radiate
into every corner of the land and over every class of
the community. The sons of landed proprietors would
leave the University and live amidst an agricultural
population. Those who would farm their own land
need a training which would enable them to use scien-
tific knowledge in improving the soil and increasing its
productiveness. To that end was established the School
of Scientiflc Agricultwre (Z'Institut Agronomigue) in
1878. It began with eight students; it has now over
200, and has a staff of eleven professors. The com-
plete cowrse is four years. Three are occupied with
what may be called agricultural engineering, including
physical, chemical, and other natural sciences, in so
far as they are applicable to farming; also a course of
social economy and law so far as rural legislation is con-
cerned. In the fourth year the courses are specialised,
to suit the purposes of students according to the sphere
of agricultural pursuits on which each is to be en-
gaged. There is a suitable museum attached to the
Institute, and laboratory work is obligatory on the
students. An important element in the course are ex-
cursions into the country, which are organised by the
different professors, and conducted by them. They are
more for business than for pleasure. The students must
join them as part of their course, must take notes of
the lectures given, which they must submit to the pro-
fessors whenever they are called for during the year. A
course of Moral Philosophy runs over the four years.
The Institute publishes the Revue Agronomique, a
monthly periodical of forty-eight pages. The work of
the Institute has been extended since its foundation,
amongst the most notable additions being a Brewery
School. There is also a School of Commercial and
Consular Sciences, the object of which is to prepare
students for a commercial or for a consular life. The
course extends over three or four years, and includes
such subjects as the following:—Commercial, Indus-
trial, and Colonial Law; Industrial and Commercial
Geography ; Diplomatic and Commercial History; the
principal European Languages and their Literatures;
also Russian and Chinese. For many years Chinese,
with other Oriental languages, had been taught; but
more as a philological study. The new school of
Chinese teaches students to speak the language, and
thus enables them to take positions which had been held
by strangers, often belonging to countries which are
commercial rivals of Belgium. Besides the advantage
such a school is to the nation at large, it opens up
consular and commercial positions to young men.

There are two other Special Schools—the Institute of
Higher Philosophy, and that of Biology and Bacterio-
logy. The former is due to the munificence of Leo
XIII; the latter is chiefly due to the late Canon Carnoy,
one of the professors. When Canon Carnoy became
professor in 1876, Louvain was without a laboratory for
Cellular Biology. He gave up apartments in his own
house for the purpose, which he furnished with appara-

tus at his own expense, and gathored his students

around him there for microscopic experiments.  In
1888 he got possession of the College de Villers for his
work. This contained his laboratories of Cytology and
Cellular Biology, and Prof. Denys’ laboratories for ex-
perimental Pathology and Bacteriology. In 1899 a
new building was opened for Bacteriology, and the old
one was devoted exclusively to Canon Carnoy’s work.

In 1882, the Belgian Bishops, at the desive of Leo
XIII. established a Chair of Thomistic Philosophy in
the University. Afterwards he determined to have several
Chairs in connection with it, and gave 150,000 francs
towards the expenses. That is the Institut Supérieur
de Plulosoplie.  The Bishops appointed Mousignor
Mercier its President, and the Belgian Catholics who.
assembled in 1891 at the Congress of Malines authorised
him to complete the work, and promised that they
would meet the cost. The Institute has fourteen pro-
fessors; and the lectures are free, and open to all.
Besides Philosophy and its History, the curriculum in-
cludes Higher Mathematics and the Natural Sciences
which bear upon philosophy; also Political and Social
Sciences. The course combines experiment and specu-
lation. Thus, to each branch of Philosophy the corres-
ponding branch of experimental science is joined:—
To Cosmology, Physics and Mathematics; to Psycho-
logy, the Natural Sciences; to Criteriology, Historical
Science; to Moral Philosophy, the Moral Sciences, and
by special application social, economic, and political
science.  The Institute has two Reviews:—ZLa Revue
Neo-Scholastique, published quarterly, and Za Revue
Sociale Catholique, published monthly.  Besides its
lecture halls, the Institute has a laboratory for physics,
psycho-physiology, and chemistry; also a library and
reading-room where over a hundred periodicals in all
the European languages are taken, amongst them
cighteen Iinglish and thirteen American periodicals.
Thirteen periodicals are published from the University
itself ; some of considerable size, and some only little
brochures.

I have on purpose kept to the present work of Lou-
vain; and I have referred only to those sciences which

THE ERASMUS SMITH ENDCWMENT.—IIIL.

L NDER the Indenture, no steps were taken to carry

into effect the trusts prescribed by it. Several
causes contributed to this. Cromwell died in the fol-
lowing year on the 3rd of September, 1658.  Under
Cromwell the whole country had been convulsed by a
scramble for the confiscated lands.  The Adventurers
cheated the soldiers, and the soldiers the Adventurers.
The Adventurers cheated one another, and the soldiers
cheated one another. The officers cheated one another,
and cheated their soldiers, whilst the Irish Catholic
landholders and all other Catholics got the choice of
hell or Connaught. All this confusion was increased by
the change of Government ; but there was no change for
the Irish Catholics.

Erasmus Smith lost no time in utilising his lease with
power to sublet. He let part of the lands which he had
given to the Trustees to Sir Charles Coote, Lord Presi-
dent of Connaught, by a lease of 41 years, dated the 9th
of December, 1657, at a rent of £300 per annum ; and
he let another part of the same lands to Richard King-
don, of Westminster, by a lease of the same length and
date, at a rent of £250 per annum (Letters Patent dated
November 3, 1667, Report of Educational Endowments
Ireland Commission, 182, pp. 190-1). Erasmus Smith
kept on his own hands the 1,467 acres in the Isles of
Arran, which he had given to the Trustees. For the
Letters Patent (ibidem) state that he had sold the Isles
of Arran to the Earl of Arran, that ¢ diverse arrears of
rent were due before the said Isles of Arran were settled
upon the said Earl of Arran, and that the said Erasmus
Smith had intended that those arrears of rent should be
paid to the said Trustées for the uses above mentioned.”’
Therefore, those arrears of rent were not arrears of rent
of the lands let to Coote and Kingdon, but were arrears
due to Erasmus Smith, which arrears it was his duty to
collect and pay to the Trustees as the whole, or part of,
the £300 per annum which he owed them. The Letters
Patent (ibid.) tell us, moreover, that instead of the
1,467 acres in the Isles of Arran, Erasmus Smith gave
to the Trustees 1,926 A. 3 . 16 r. in the Barony of
Coonagh, Co. Limerick, which are specifically mentioned
in the Letters Patent; and, on account of the 1,467
acres in the Isles of Arran which were taken from the
Trustees, £130 per annum were knocked off the £300
per annum which he had, by lease, undertaken to pay
them.

P - THE LEADER.

Avcust 12, 1905.

We have thus data to determine up to a certain
amount the profits which Erasmus Smith realised for him-
self from the beginning out of the 2,876 A. 3 r. which
he had given for the Endowment. Ho received from
Coote and Kingdon £550 a year for the lands which he
had leased to them. He held, as I have shown, on his
own hands, the lands and rents of the 1,467 acres in the
Isles of Arran. Since £130 per annum were knocked
off the £300 per annum which Erasmus Smith had
undertaken to pay the Trustees, we may take the pro-
portion of the letting value of the 1,467 acres in the
TIsles of Arran to the letting value of the lands leased to
Coote and Kingdon as 130 to 170. - At this proportion
the letting value of the 1,467 acres in the Tsles of Ar-
ran should be £420 per annum. Thus Erasmus Smith
received £970 per annum out of the lands, whilst he
paid the Tvustees only £300 per annum. Bub his pro-
fits did not stop here. As we have seen, his own lease
and rent did not begin to run until the lst of May,
1658, whilst the leases to Coote and Kingdon began to
run on the 9th of December, 1657. Thus he secured
five months’ rent or £228 before his obligation to pay
began, so that his profits within the first year were
£898. But this sum did not represent his full profits.
For £420 per annum for the land in the Isles of Arran
only represented the rent which Coote or Kingdon would
pay for it. And as they made a profit on their leases,
Erasmus Smith screwed out of the tenants in the Isles
of Arran more than £420 a year. Moreover, the In-
denture tells us that Erasmus Smith reserved to himself
all cyries of hawks in the Isles of Arran, and the right
to dig, polish, and carry away marbles from the lands
in Arran. So that his profits on the 2,876 acres which
he had given to his Endowment was not far short, if it
was anything short, of £800 a year.

“Being on’’ bounty “bent he had a frugal mind.”
This throws a lurid light on the statement of Lord Jus-
tice Fitzgibbon “ that Erasmus Smith was an earnest
and enthusiastic Protestant who wished to propagate his
creed by his schools.”” We shall see later on that he
had no creed of his own to propagate; that he always
took his creed from the powers that ruled.

As Erasmus Smith had secured to himself out of the
lands which he had given to his foundation, nearly three
times as much of the rents as he had given to the Trus-
tees, and did not, as I have shown, pay the £300 per
annum punctually to the Trustees, the money only
slowly accumulated for building the schools; and this
was an additional cause for not carrying the Trusts into
cffect soon after their foundation. But the principal
cause of delay was, that Erasmus Smith had used his
school-building purpose as a means of acquiring large
tracts—thousands of acres—of the best lands in Ire-
land. TFor this purpose, he retained to himself, in the
Indenture, the right to change the sites of the five
schools, four of which he had fixed; for this purpose
he had fixed the site of only one school in the Letters
Patent of 1667, and reserved to himself the right to fix
the sites of the other two schools therein mentioned.
He afterwards fixed the sites of these two schools at Tip-
perary and Drogheda, around which places he had sub-
sequently acquired large tracts of the best lands in Ire-
land. His game evidently was to seek the acquisition
of the best lands in Ireland on the plea of founding
schools on them. After he had acquired title to all his
ostates in Ireland, and before a penny was expended on
exhibitions to scholars in Trinity College, or on cloth-
ing poor scholars in the schools, or apprenticing them,
he sought to take away to Christ’s Hospital, London, a
large portion, probably one-third of the rents of the En-
dowment. This is proved by two letters, one of the
Trustees to Erasmus Smith, and the other of Erasmus
Smith to the Trustees, dated the 7th of June, 1681, and
the 6th of Awugust, 1681, respectively (Rep. 1885-6,
pp. 439-40).

And he had taken this action, although he had stated
in the beginning of the Chaxrter of 1669, that two of the
reasons for founding 3, instead of 5, Free Grammar
Schools were to reserve “ money for the clothing of poor
scholars in the schools, and binding them out appren-
tices.””  He had reserved to himself in the Chaiter a
power of devoting to other charitable purposes, a moiety

POEMS—LE FANU.

$ LTHOUGH Le Fanu will be always better known
‘ % through his prose rather than through his
poetry, I am certain that this book will receive a wel-
come from many—particularly those interested in the
literary movement of the Irish Revival. To my mind
' Le Fanu’s poems have not received that amount of at-
tenfion which their merit deserves. To some degree,
ab any rate, this is probably due to the fact that until

URRAY'S

409

a comparatively recent date they had not been collected
and published in one volume. Now, however, this cir-
cumstance is removed, and with Messrs. Duffy’s publi-
cation of the way has been made clear for the many
becoming possessed of Le Fanu’s poetry in convenient
form.

This little volume includes * Beatrice,”” “The Le-
gend of the Glaive,”” ‘“Shamus O’Brien,” “Paudrig
Crohoore,”’ and a half-dozen others.

The most ambitious of these is  Beatrice ’’—a drama
showing traces of a dramatic talent which there is rea-
son to regret was not more studiously cultivated by its
owner. The well-known atmosphere of the old Irish
legendary lore is given us in picturesque language in
“The ILegend of the Glaive;’’ whilst in  Shamus
O’Brien ”’ the trial and escape of an Irish rebel is told
in that casy, flowing style which has earned much popu-
larity for the poem.

Le Fanu was a diligent student of Spiritualism, but
it has not influenced his work. He was no mystic—
for which reason, perhaps, Mr. Yeats would relegate
him to “exterior darkness.”” Even the mystics, how-
ever, will, I am sure, admit that there is much beauty
in Le Fanu’s poems. He had not, of course, the music
of Moore, nor the strange genius-spark of Mangan, but
his verses have very frequently a prettiness all their own
to which every lover of poetry must give his admiration.

This volume is nicely turned out by Messrs. Jas.
Duffy and Co., Wellington Quay, Dublin, and sells
for 2s. CLADDAGH.
